wasps.desc: Aggressive ground-nesting yellowjacket. Most common nuisance wasp at outdoor food.
Female has ovipositor longer than body — drills through wood to parasitize horntail larvae.
Fast-flying wasp that digs burrows in sand. Provisions nest with flies for larvae.
Black wasp with blue wings and orange abdominal spots. Parasitizes Japanese beetle grubs.
Large wood wasp (sawfly). Female drills into hardwoods to lay eggs. Hosts giant ichneumons.
Solitary black and white wasp. Nests in old carpenter bee galleries. Provisions with caterpillars.
Brown paper wasp. Builds small open-face nests under eaves. Less aggressive than yellowjackets.
Metallic blue wasp that hunts crickets. Stings and paralyzes prey, drags to burrow.
Aggressive invasive ant with painful sting. Mound-building. Spreading through SoCal.
Tiny dark ant smelling of coconut when crushed. Most common indoor ant pest.
Dark ant nesting in dead wood and tree cavities. Produces formic acid. Forms long trails.
Close relative of L. occidentale. Found in mountain areas on oaks and conifers.

trueBugs.desc: Bright green shield bug. Found on many plants. Emits pungent defensive odor.
Dark brown stink bug with orange/red border. Agricultural pest of beans and tomatoes.
Black and red scentless plant bug. Aggregates on sun-warmed walls in fall. On boxelder maples.
Tiny bug with lace-like transparent wings. Sucks sap from leaf undersides. Many host-specific species.
Tiny predator — eats thrips, mites, aphid eggs. Important biocontrol agent. Can bite humans.
Green-brown plant bug. Major agricultural pest of strawberries, cotton, alfalfa.
Delicate long-legged bug on vegetation. Predatory — feeds on small insects and eggs.
Large aquatic predator to 60mm. Ambushes fish and frogs. Painful bite. 'Toe-biter.' Attracted to lights.
Skates on water surface film using hydrophobic leg hairs. Detects ripples from trapped insects.
Orange and black seed bug on milkweed. Sequesters cardiac glycosides. Model organism in biology.
Dark blue-black bug with orange border. Nymphs bright red-purple. On various plants.
Actually a true bug mimic. Tiny cryptic planthopper perfectly matching creosote leaves.

flies.desc: Large bee fly that parasitizes carpenter bees. Flicks eggs into bee nests.
Very large black and orange fly mimicking spider wasps. Larvae predatory in soil.
Small fly with ornate wing patterns. Displays wings in courtship dances on leaves.
Large hover fly whose larvae develop in rotting cactus. Bee mimic. Technically a hoverfly.
Tiny black fly in houseplant soil. Larvae feed on fungus and roots. Common indoor nuisance.
Fuzzy moth-like fly in bathrooms and drains. Larvae develop in organic films in pipes.
Major quarantine pest of fruit. Periodic detections trigger aerial spraying in LA.
Black soldier fly — larvae efficiently convert organic waste. Used in composting and animal feed.
Predatory fly of sandy habitats. Larvae hunt soil-dwelling insect larvae. Covered in golden hairs.
Metallic green bottle fly. Important in forensic entomology. Larvae used in medical maggot therapy.
Gray fly that overwinters in buildings in clusters. Larvae are earthworm parasites.
Blood-feeding fly that bites livestock and humans. Breeds in decaying vegetation, not manure.

hoverflies.desc: Tiny hoverfly with yellow and black abdomen. Larvae eat aphids. One of most common garden hoverflies.
Large bee-mimicking hoverfly. Rat-tailed maggot larvae in stagnant water.
European hoverfly now common in NA. Honeybee mimic. Larvae in polluted water.
Yellow and black banded hoverfly near wetlands. Rat-tailed larvae in muddy water.
Small dark hoverfly with yellow lunules on abdomen. Larvae are voracious aphid predators.
Slender hoverfly with oblique yellow markings. Excellent aphid biocontrol. Hovers at flowers.

nativeBees.desc: Metallic blue mason bee. Excellent fruit pollinator — more efficient than honeybees per bee. Nests in tubes.
Small gray bee that cuts circular leaf pieces to line nest cells. Important alfalfa pollinator.
Diverse genus of ground-nesting bees. Many species active in early spring. Important native pollinators.
Specialist bee of prickly pear cactus. Ground-nesting. Covered in pale hairs.
Specialist of cucurbit flowers. Active before dawn when squash blooms open. Ground-nesting.
Uses plant resins instead of leaf pieces to build nest. Cavity nester in wood and stems.
European bee that scrapes plant hairs for nest. Males territorial — ram other bees in flight.
Males have extremely long antennae. Fast-flying. Ground-nesting. Important sunflower pollinator.
Brilliant metallic green bee. Females iridescent all over; males green head with striped abdomen.
Small brilliant green bee nesting in rotting wood — unusual for a sweat bee. Solitary.

orthoptera.desc: Most abundant grasshopper in California grasslands. Periodic outbreaks cause crop damage.
Major crop pest across western NA. Can form loose swarms during outbreaks. Brown with red hind tibiae.
Large green grasshopper to 60mm. Related to desert locust. Feeds on many plants.
Large bright green grasshopper of desert margins. Strong flier. On creosote and mesquite.
Spectacular black, red, yellow, and blue grasshopper. Flightless — relies on warning coloration. Desert margins.
Humpbacked cave/camel cricket. Nocturnal. Found in garages, basements, under rocks.
Green katydid with long wings. Males stridulate at night. On shrubs and garden plants.
Common garden earwig with rear pincers. Nocturnal omnivore. Female guards eggs — rare maternal care in insects.

snails.desc: Large white snail from Mediterranean. Established in disturbed coastal areas. Edible species.
Small brown snail with dark spiral band. Garden pest. Introduced from East Asia.
Variable shell color and banding. Classic subject of evolutionary studies on natural selection.
Translucent flattened snail. Predatory — eats other snails and slugs. Under wood and leaf litter.
Native land snail with keeled shell. Found in coastal scrub and chaparral. Declining.
Large spotted slug to 15cm. Spectacular aerial mating — pair hangs from mucus thread. Introduced.
Dark keeled slug. Underground feeder damaging roots and tubers. Serious garden pest.

myriapods.desc: Variable colored centipede to 15cm. Under rocks in dry habitats. Venomous bite — painful but not dangerous.
Fast-running centipede with extremely long legs. Beneficial predator indoors. Eats spiders and roaches.
Small dark millipede common in gardens and greenhouses. Feeds on decaying plant matter.
Large dark millipede to 10cm. Curls into spiral when disturbed. Detritivore in leaf litter.

isopods.desc: Large fast isopod of rocky seashores. Runs when disturbed. Feeds on algae and detritus.
Gray oval isopod under logs and stones. Cannot roll into ball (unlike pillbugs). European introduction.
Fast-running woodlouse with dark dorsal stripe. In leaf litter and compost. European.
Large beach hopper to 25mm. Burrows in sand above tide line. Emerges at night to feed on kelp wrack.

dragonflies.desc: Large blue darner with paddle-shaped cerci. Mountain lakes and ponds. Powerful flier.
Large damselfly endemic to California. Oviposits into willow bark over streams.
Males powder-blue, females green. Aggressive pond dragonfly that hunts other odonates.
River specialist with pale face. Strong fast flight over rocky streams. Rarely perches.
